title: The Task Planner
concept: Prompting a model to decompose a user request into a linear list of independent sub-tasks.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: in a plan-and-execute agent, what does the PLANNER do?

options.text: It makes the whole plan upfront, before any step runs
It runs each step one at a time and checks the result
It waits until the very end to decide what to do

exercises.explain: The planner's job is to think ahead and lay out the full plan first. A separate worker then executes those steps one by one.

cards.title: Big job, tiny pieces
cards.body: Imagine asking an agent to 'plan a birthday party.' That's huge! Before doing anything, a smart agent breaks it into a clear list of smaller jobs.

cards.title: What is a task planner?
cards.body: A task planner is a prompt that asks the model to turn one big request into a numbered list of smaller sub-tasks. Each sub-task is a single, clear step the worker can do.

cards.title: Like a shopping list
cards.body: Instead of remembering 'get everything for dinner,' you write a list: buy pasta, buy sauce, buy cheese. Each line is one simple task you can check off.

cards.title: Planning a trip
cards.body: Request: 'Plan my weekend trip.' The planner outputs: 1) Pick a city, 2) Book a hotel, 3) Buy train tickets, 4) List things to pack. Four clear steps in order.

cards.title: Not one giant blob
cards.body: Myth: a good plan is one long paragraph. Nope! A good plan is a linear list where each item is separate and doesn't overlap with the others.

cards.title: Vague vs. broken down
cards.body: The planner turns a fuzzy goal into neat, doable steps.

right.text: 1) Pick a topic 2) Choose a name 3) Write first post

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of a task planner prompt?

options.text: To break a big request into a clear list of smaller sub-tasks
To answer the user in one long sentence
To skip planning and start acting immediately
To make the request sound more complicated

exercises.explain: A task planner's whole point is decomposition: taking one big goal and splitting it into small, ordered steps a worker can actually do.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a good task plan, each sub-task should be a single, clear step.
exercises.explain: Yes! Each sub-task should be one clear, doable step so the worker knows exactly what to do without confusion.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about what the planner produces.
exercises.sentence: The task planner turns one big request into a numbered list of smaller sub-tasks.
exercises.explain: The planner breaks the request down into sub-tasks: small, separate steps that together complete the big goal.

exercises.prompt: A user asks: 'Plan a study schedule for my exam.' Which response looks like an AI task planner breaking it into sub-tasks?

options.text: 1) List all exam topics, 2) Rank topics by difficulty, 3) Assign each topic to a day, 4) Schedule review sessions
Good luck on your exam, you've got this!
Studying is really important for doing well in school.

exercises.explain: The AI planner output is the numbered list of clear, independent sub-tasks. The others are just encouragement or a general comment, not a plan.

exercises.prompt: You prompt a planner: 'Break this request into a linear list of independent sub-tasks: Make a poster for the school fair.' Which output best matches what a task planner should return?

options.text: 1) Decide the poster's message, 2) Pick colors and images, 3) Lay out the text, 4) Print copies
Posters are a fun way to advertise events at school.
Just make it look nice and colorful, that's all you need.
Step 1: do everything needed to finish the whole poster at once.

exercises.explain: A task planner decomposes a big request into a linear list of clear, independent sub-tasks in order. Option A does exactly that. B is just a comment, C is vague, and D crams everything into one un-broken-down step, which defeats the purpose of planning.
